item.title: The Grand Canal, Venice
item.museum: Fine Arts Museums of San Francisco
item.lesson: Of course Monet had to face it eventually: the Grand Canal itself, with the Salute — the church that has haunted this whole exhibition — floating at the end like a recurring dream. He painted this view six times from the steps of the Palazzo Barbaro, always at the same hour, the mooring poles tilting across the foreground like a bar of music. Compare it with where we began. Canaletto's canal was a measured avenue; Monet's is a body of trembling violet light that happens to have a church in it. The dome does not sit on the water — it rises out of the haze the water makes, stone and steam become the same substance. This particular canvas crossed an ocean and a continent long ago; of the six versions, this is the one that came to rest in San Francisco. Monet kept the Venice pictures in his studio for four years, retouching them from memory, unwilling to let the city go. When they finally showed in Paris in 1912, even his rivals conceded: the man had gone to the most-painted city on earth and found it unpainted.
item.lookFor: Count the blues between the poles in the foreground — violet, turquoise, near-white, a green like old glass. Canaletto used one blue for a whole sky; Monet spends a dozen on a few feet of water.
